mission.title: Install and Configure Fluid on Kubernetes
mission.description: Fluid is an open-source Kubernetes-native Distributed Dataset Orchestrator and Accelerator designed for data-intensive applications such as big data and AI. Installing Fluid allows you to efficiently manage and accelerate data processing in cloud environments.

steps.title: Add the Fluid Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Fluid Helm repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add fluid https://fluid-cloudnative.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Fluid using Helm
steps.description:
Install Fluid with the specified version and create a namespace for it.
```bash
helm install fluid fluid/fluid --version 1.0.8 --namespace fluid-system-system --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check the status of the Fluid pods to ensure they are running.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace fluid-system
```

steps.title: Verify CRDs are installed
steps.description:
Verify that the Fluid CRDs have been registered:
```bash
kubectl get crd | grep fluid
```

You should see CRDs like `datasets.data.fluid.io` and various runtime CRDs.

uninstall.title: Remove the Fluid Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Fluid release from your cluster.
```bash
helm uninstall fluid --namespace fluid-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
uninstall.description:
Remove all Fluid CRDs to clean up resources.
```bash
kubectl delete crd datasets.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd alluxioruntimes.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd databackups.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd dataloads.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd jindoruntimes.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd goosefsruntimes.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd juicefsruntimes.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd thinruntimes.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd thinruntimeprofiles.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd efcruntimes.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd datamigrates.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd dataprocesses.data.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd vineyardruntimes.data.fluid.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Fluid namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the Fluid namespace to complete the uninstallation.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace fluid
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Backup the current Fluid state before upgrading.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace fluid-system -o yaml > fluid-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Ensure your Helm repository is up to date.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Fluid to the latest version
upgrade.description:
Upgrade Fluid to the latest version available in the Helm repository.
```bash
helm upgrade fluid fluid/fluid --namespace fluid-system-system
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the Fluid pods to ensure they are running after the upgrade.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace fluid-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending
troubleshooting.description:
This may indicate insufficient resources in the cluster. Check the events for the pods:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace fluid-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This indicates that the pods are failing to start. Check the logs for more information:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace fluid-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the service is not reachable, verify the service configuration:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace fluid-system
kubectl describe svc <service-name> --namespace fluid-system
```

troubleshooting.title: CRDs not found
troubleshooting.description:
Ensure that the CRDs are installed correctly. List the CRDs:
```bash
kubectl get crd | grep fluid
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Fluid will have all pods running in the 'fluid' namespace, and you will be able to access the Fluid services as needed. You can verify this by checking the pod status and service endpoints.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add fluid https://fluid-cloudnative.github.io/charts
helm repo update

kubectl edit deployment fluid --namespace fluid-system
# Set resource limits under spec.containers.resources

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and Helm installed.
